defects.defect_text: Progressive tooth flank wear on gearbox gears with increased backlash and audible gear rattle.
Gearbox bearing temperature rising above normal with grease extrusion and discoloration.
Oil leaking from gearbox shaft seal forming drip/pool beneath gearbox and oil-level drop.
Shaft runout and increased vibration consistent with gearbox shaft misalignment.
Gearbox breather or oil filter clogged with restricted oil flow and increased differential pressure.
Visible scoring or grooves on pilger roll surface causing surface marks on produced tube.
Galling between roll and mandrel surfaces producing heat and transfer material onto tube OD/ID.
High vibration peaks at roll-bearing locations under load, audible whine during pass.
Crack in roll groove/die insert found after a pass with abrupt change in roll noise.
Eccentric tube geometry and non-uniform wall reduction indicating roll-stack misalignment.
Mandrel profile wear visible as loss of diameter tolerance and poor ID finish.
Mandrel chuck slipping or losing clamping torque evidenced by mandrel rotation or chatter.
Mandrel bearing seizing with sudden jamming of mandrel feed and spike in drive torque.
Mandrel deflection observed with eccentric ID or repeatable offset between passes.
Leakage at mandrel seal leading to lubricant ingress/egress near chuck region.
Hydraulic pressure oscillations at the feed/actuator supply causing inconsistent rolling force.
Hydraulic pump emits cavitation noise with reduced flow under nominal speed.
External hydraulic oil leak from hose or fitting with visible fluid trail and pressure drop.
Accumulator gas/urea leak or bladders loss resulting in reduced hydraulic damping.
Directional valve sticking or slow response causing delayed feed/clamp movement.
Lubrication pump flow reduced; dry contact marks on roll or mandrel surfaces observed.
Clogged lubrication system filter with pressure differential and reduced lubricant delivery.
Lubricant contaminated with metal particles visible in filter/sight-glass and increased wear.
Die/roll lubrication nozzle blocked causing dry patches on tooling faces.
Lubrication pump noisy under load indicating cavitation or bearing distress.
PLC registers position/speed sensor fault causing aborts or incorrect cycle sequencing.
Encoder miscounts producing incorrect mandrel/feed positioning and inconsistent pass lengths.
HMI/software fault causing unexpected screen freezes or loss of operator control during cycle.
VFD fault trip reported on drive panel causing drive stop or torque interruption.
Loss of synchronization between roll reciprocation and feed indexing causing mis-passes.
Feed carriage jams or stalls during travel with audible grinding or abrupt stop.
Chuck jaw wear resulting in insufficient grip and billet/mandrel slip during pass.
Feed drive motor trips on overload indicated by thermal trip or current spike.
Feed slide seizure due to lubrication starvation with abrupt motion halt.
Feed actuator hose leak producing loss of clamp/feed force and visible seepage.
Main drive motor bearing failure with noise, heat, and axial/radial shaft play.
Drive coupling damage or fracture producing sudden loss of power transmission and shock loads.
VFD produces electrical fault (overcurrent/overvoltage) causing intermittent drive interruptions.
Imbalance in rotating components causing elevated bearing temperatures and vibration.
Coolant pump leak or hose rupture with loss of coolant flow to dies/rolls.
Coolant contaminated with tramp oil or fines causing poor cooling and surface staining.
Cooling circuit blockage in headers or nozzles reducing flow to tooling.
Excessive tooling or roll temperature despite nominal coolant flow.
Machine guard fasteners loosened or missing allowing unsafe access during operation.
Safety interlock fails to stop motion or permits start with guard open.
E-stop circuit fault or wiring failure resulting in non-functional emergency stop.
Safety sensor (light curtain/door) malfunction causing false trips or no trip.
Die/roll groove chipping or micro-fractures observed after a pass with change in sound.
Roll profile wear producing out-of-tolerance tube OD and poor surface finish.
Tooling fastener backing off causing run-out or shifting of roll/die insert.
Incorrect tooling mounting leading to non-uniform pass deformation and edge marks.
Excessive vibration measured across sensors that increases with spindle reciprocation frequency.
Localized temperature rise detected at bearing or gearbox thermocouple above set threshold.
Oil analysis point shows elevated ferrous particle counts and abnormal wear metals.
Encoder or position sensor intermittent signal causing missed counts or jitter.
Dominant vibration frequency shift indicating structural resonance or looseness.

subsystems.description: Gearbox and main transmission reduction assembly driving reciprocating motion (gears, shafts, bearings, seals).
Roll stand and roll/die assembly performing the cold pilger reduction passes and roll guidance.
Mandrel system including mandrel bar, bearings, chucks and mounting for internal support and ID sizing.
Hydraulic system for feed, clamp and actuation (pumps, valves, accumulators, piping).
Central lubrication and die-lubrication circuits supplying oil to bearings, gears and roll faces.
Control electronics and software stack (PLC, HMI, VFDs, I/O, safety logic).
Feed carriage, slides, chucks and drive mechanisms that position billet/mandrel between passes.
Main powertrain and electrical drive components (motors, couplings, VFDs, starters).
Cooling system for tooling and rolls including pumps, headers, nozzles and return lines.
Safety and guarding systems (interlocks, e-stops, light curtains, guards).
Forming tooling cluster: roll/die inserts, holders, fasteners and mounting interfaces.
Condition monitoring and instrumentation (vibration, temperature, oil analysis, encoders).

components.description: Spur/helical gears in reduction gearbox and drive trains transmitting reciprocating motion.
Rolling-element and plain bearings used on mandrels, rolls, gearbox and motor shafts.
Pilger/form rolls and roll inserts that shape tube OD and form passes.
Mandrel bar/profile providing internal support and ID finishing during reduction.
Central lubrication pump supplying oil to gearbox, bearings and roll face nozzles.
Directional, servo and pressure control valves in hydraulic feed and clamp circuits.
Programmable logic controller executing sequences, interlocks and motion coordination.
Electric motors (main drive and feed) often coupled to VFDs for torque/speed control.
Shaft seals, lip seals and static seals for gearbox, mandrel chucks and hydraulic actuators.
Billet and mandrel chucks and jaws used for gripping and indexing stock.
Feed carriage, slides and linear guides that translate billets/mandrels for each pass.
Lubrication and hydraulic filters/removal elements protecting fluid systems from particles.
Coolant circulation pump and headers that supply die/roll cooling.
Rotary and linear encoders for accurate position feedback of mandrel and feed axes.
Replaceable die/roll groove inserts and form tooling which contact the workpiece.
High-pressure hydraulic and coolant hoses and flexible connections.
Mechanical couplings between motors, gearboxes and shafts transmitting torque.
Variable frequency drives controlling motor speed/torque for main and feed motors.
Hydraulic accumulators used for pressure damping and energy storage in feed circuits.
Drive and mandrel shafts transmitting rotation and reciprocation loads.
Emergency stop switches and wiring used for machine shut-down safety.
Thermocouples or RTD sensors used for local temperature monitoring (gearbox/bearing).
Designated oil sampling/analysis points used for fluid health monitoring.
Bolts, studs and clamps used to secure tooling, bearings and structural members.
Bearing housings and mounts that locate bearings and transmit load to structure.

defect_types.description: Progressive material removal or profile loss on rolls, mandrel or gears due to friction/abrasion.
Visible fracture or crack in tooling, structural members or rotating parts from fatigue/overload.
Loss of preload or fastening integrity causing relative movement or play.
Hydraulic, lubricant or coolant fluid escaping from seals, fittings or hoses.
Elevated local temperature in bearings, gearbox or tooling leading to lubricant breakdown.
Geometric misalignment between rolls, mandrel and drives causing eccentricity or uneven wear.
Surface scoring, galling, imprinting or marks transferred from tooling to tube surface.
Contamination of lubricants/coolants with particles, tramp oil or metal debris.
Excessive dynamic excitation degrading finish and component life.
Hydraulic pressure instability or pump cavitation affecting feed/clamp forces.
Restricted fluid flow due to clogged filters reducing lubrication/cooling efficacy.
Failure or incorrect reading from encoders, thermos or position sensors impacting control.
Unexpected blocking or locking of a bearing, slide or mandrel due to lubrication loss or overload.
Adhesive wear and material transfer between tooling and workpiece generating burrs/marks.
Material failure from cyclic loading presenting as cracks or progressive fracture.
Chemical or galvanic attack causing pitting or material loss on tooling or structures.
Obstruction in coolant/lube passages or nozzles reducing fluid delivery.
Rotational imbalance causing excessive vibration and bearing loads.
Loss of frictional engagement (chuck/jaw slip) resulting in relative motion during pass.
Electrical faults in drives, VFDs or wiring causing trips or degraded performance.
Software/control logic faults producing unexpected resets, sequence errors or wrong setpoints.

meta.notes: This taxonomy was produced by consolidating OEM equipment descriptions (SMS, LG-series suppliers), industry maintenance articles (The Fabricator), bearing and failure case studies (NSK), and targeted academic diagnostics for Pilger mills. Definitions and defect types were selected for high relevance to cold Pilger (HSP) reciprocating roll-and-mandrel systems. All defect codes follow PLG.<SUBSYS>.<COMPONENT>.<DEFECT_TYPE> where tokens match the flat arrays. Users should validate manufacturer-specific part numbers and intervals against the OEM manual; machine variations across vendors may require minor mapping adjustments.
